message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: ADORE's 52.65 was measured on the LEGACY Gemini-2.5-Pro judge, in a January 2026 paper, and its '#1 on the leaderboard' claim is now ~18 months of leaderboard-churn stale. Do NOT treat 52.65 as SOTA-on-our-evaluator.
DETAIL: arXiv 2601.18267 ('Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG', PDF footer 'Generated on Mon Jan 26 08:48:29 2026') Table 2 lists: 1 ADORE 52.65/52.22/54.37/51.11/52.18; 2 Tavily Research 52.44/52.84/53.59/51.92/49.21; 3 ThinkDepthAI 52.43/52.02/53.88/52.04/50.12; 4 CellCog 51.94; 5 Salesforce Air 50.65/50.00/51.09/50.77/50.32; 6 LangChain(GPT-5) 50.60; 7 Gemini 2.5 Pro 49.71; 8 LangChain(Tavily) 49.33; 9 OpenAI-DR 46.45; 10 Claude Research 45.00. Every one of those baseline rows is a byte-for-byte match to the CURRENT Gemini-2.5-Pro-judged CSV (tavily-research 52.44/52.84/53.59/51.92/49.21; thinkdepthai 52.43; salesforce-air 50.65; gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60; gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.71; openai-deepresearch 46.45). That proves ADORE scored against the Gemini-2.5-Pro leaderboard, months before GPT-5.5 existed as the judge. The paper never names its judge model — it just cites the bench.
SYSTEM: ADORE
SOURCE GIVEN: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt: What is the title, authors, date of this paper? Does it mention ADORE, DeepResearch Bench, RACE scores?

input.query: arXiv 2601.18267 ADORE Orchestrating Specialized Agents Trustworthy Enterprise RAG

content.content:
**Title:** "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG"

**Authors:** Xincheng You, Qi Sun, Neha Bora, Huayi Li, Shubham Goel, Kang Li, and Sean Culatana

**Submission Date:** January 26, 2026

**Mentions of requested terms:**

Yes, the paper explicitly discusses ADORE and the evaluation benchmarks. The abstract describes ADORE as "an agentic framework that replaces linear retrieval with iterative, user-steered investigation." 

Regarding performance metrics, the abstract reports that "ADORE ranks first on DeepResearch Bench (52.65)" and achieves "the highest head-to-head preference win rate on DeepConsult (77.2%)." However, the document does not mention RACE scores.

Web search results for query: "arXiv 2601.18267 ADORE Orchestrating Specialized Agents Trustworthy Enterprise RAG"

Links: [{"title":"[2601.18267] Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18267"},{"title":"Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG Xincheng You","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.18267"},{"title":"Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2601.18267"},{"title":"Uno-Orchestra: Parsimonious Agent Routing via Selective Delegation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05007"},{"title":"Experience as a Compass: Multi-agent RAG with Evolving Orchestration and Agent Prompts","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.00901"},{"title":"Orchestrating Agents and Data for Enterprise: A Blueprint ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2504.08148"},{"title":"Agentic AI with Orchestrator-Agent Trust: A Modular Visual Classification Framework with Trust-Aware Orchestration and RAG-Based Reasoning","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.10571"},{"title":"Retrieval-Conditioned Topology Selection with Provable Budget Conservation for Multi-Agent Code Generation","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.05657"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about arXiv paper 2601.18267:

## ADORE: Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG

ADORE (Adaptive Deep Orchestration for Research in Enterprise) is an agentic framework that replaces linear retrieval with iterative, user-steered investigation coordinated by a central orchestrator and a set of specialized agents.

### Key Problem
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) shows promise for enterprise knowledge work, yet it often underperforms in high-stakes decision settings that require deep synthesis, strict traceability, and recovery from underspecified prompts. One-pass retrieval-and-write pipelines frequently yield shallow summaries, inconsistent grounding, and weak mechanisms for completeness verification.

### Core Contributions
ADORE's approach includes: (1) Memory-locked synthesis - report generation constrained to a structured Memory Bank with explicit claim-evidence linkage; (2) Evidence-coverage-guided execution - a retrieval-reflection loop audits section-level evidence coverage to trigger targeted follow-up retrieval; and (3) Section-packed long-context grounding - section-level packing and citation-preserving compression for long-form synthesis under context limits.

### Design Philosophy
ADORE replaces linear "retrieve-then-write" with an agentic workflow that mirrors human research: scoping via clarification, plan formation, iterative evidence collection, and verification.

The paper was published on January 26, 2026, and is available on arXiv.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

input.prompt: Reproduce Table 2 exactly (the DeepResearch Bench leaderboard table) with all rows and columns. Also: does the paper anywhere name the judge/evaluator LLM used for DeepResearch Bench (e.g. Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-5, GPT-5.5)? Quote any sentence describing the RACE evaluation setup or judge model. Also state what date/snapshot of the leaderboard they claim.

content.content:
# Table 2 Reproduction: DeepResearch Bench RACE Scores

| Rank | Model | Overall | Comp. | Insight | Inst. | Read. |
|------|-------|---------|-------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | ADORE (Ours) | 52.65 | 52.22 | 54.37 | 51.11 | 52.18 |
| 2 | Tavily Research | 52.44 | 52.84 | 53.59 | 51.92 | 49.21 |
| 3 | ThinkDepthAI | 52.43 | 52.02 | 53.88 | 52.04 | 50.12 |
| 4 | CellCog | 51.94 | 52.17 | 51.90 | 51.37 | 51.94 |
| 5 | Salesforce Air | 50.65 | 50.00 | 51.09 | 50.77 | 50.32 |
| 6 | LangChain (GPT-5) | 50.60 | 50.06 | 50.76 | 51.31 | 49.72 |
| 7 | Gemini 2.5 Pro | 49.71 | 49.51 | 49.45 | 50.12 | 50.00 |
| 8 | LangChain (Tavily) | 49.33 | 49.80 | 47.34 | 51.05 | 48.99 |
| 9 | OpenAI Deep Research | 46.45 | 46.46 | 43.73 | 49.39 | 47.22 |
| 10 | Claude Research | 45.00 | 45.34 | 42.79 | 47.58 | 44.66 |

## Judge Model Identification

**The paper does not explicitly name the evaluator LLM used for DeepResearch Bench RACE scoring.** 

The only methodological description states: "For a given research task T, RACE generates a weight vector W_T tailored to the specific requirements of the prompt." However, **no sentence specifies which judge model performed this evaluation.**

**Leaderboard snapshot:** The paper references "Du et al., [2025]" as the source but does not specify a particular date or snapshot.

Links: [{"title":"Gemini 2.5 Pro vs GPT-5.5 Pro: AI Benchmark Comparison 2026 | BenchLM.ai","url":"https://benchlm.ai/compare/gemini-2-5-pro-vs-gpt-5-5-pro"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.11763"},{"title":"DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents","url":"https://deepresearch-bench.github.io/"},{"title":"GitHub - Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench"},{"title":"AI Model Leaderboard 2026: GPT-5, Claude, Gemini ...","url":"https://localaimaster.com/tools/ai-model-leaderboard"},{"title":"SWE-rebench Leaderboard","url":"https://swe-rebench.com/"},{"title":"LLM Benchmark Leaderboard 2026: Coding, Math, Reasoning and Agents | CodeSOTA | CodeSOTA","url":"https://www.codesota.com/llm"},{"title":"FinDeepResearch: Evaluating Deep Research Agents in Rigorous Financial Analysis","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.13936"},{"title":"AI Model Leaderboard July 2026 — LMSys Arena, LLM, Image & Coding Rankings | Swfte","url":"https://www.swfte.com/ai/leaderboard"},{"title":"RhinoInsight: Improving Deep Research through Control Mechanisms for Model Behavior and Context","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.18743"}]

Based on the search results, I found information about DeepResearch Bench and RACE framework, though the specific details about judge replacement vary:

The DeepResearch Bench evaluation infrastructure was upgraded with Gemini-2.5-Pro for RACE evaluation and Gemini-2.5-Flash for FACT evaluation, and a GPT-5.5 leaderboard was still under construction as of the time referenced in the GitHub repository.

Legacy code for the previous Gemini-2.5-Pro / Gemini-2.5-Flash evaluation code is preserved on the Gemini-2.5 branch, indicating that there have been changes to the evaluation infrastructure over time.

Regarding the RACE framework results, Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research demonstrated leading overall performance (48.88) across all dimensions in the original DeepResearch Bench evaluation.

However, the search results don't provide specific information about GPT-5.5 replacing Gemini-2.5-Pro as the judge in the RACE framework for the 2026 leaderboard. The repository indicates changes were made to evaluation infrastructure, but the details about which model currently serves as the judge and when this transition occurred are not clearly detailed in these search results.

input.prompt: What judge model is currently used for RACE evaluation? Was Gemini-2.5-Pro replaced by GPT-5.5? Any dates for the change? What are the top leaderboard entries and does ADORE appear?

content.content:
# DeepResearch Bench Judge Model Update

**Current RACE Judge:** Yes, Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5.

**Timeline:** According to the announcement, "Following Google's announced June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro," the benchmark switched to GPT-5.5 for RACE evaluation (with GPT-5.4-mini for FACT). The change was announced on May 11, 2026, with a "dual-acceptance window" extending through May 31, 2026, allowing submissions under either evaluator. Full migration to the new system occurred by June 1, 2026.

**Leaderboard Entry Note:** The document references a live leaderboard at Hugging Face but does not display the actual top entries or mention ADORE specifically. To see current rankings and specific model performance, you would need to visit the hosted leaderboard directly.

content.text:
Verification complete. Findings:

**Primary source (arXiv 2601.18267):** Confirmed. Title "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG" (You, Sun, Bora, Li, Goel, Li, Culatana), submitted Jan 26, 2026. Abstract: "ADORE ranks first on DeepResearch Bench (52.65)" and "77.2%" on DeepConsult. Table 2 reproduces exactly as the claim states, including all five columns of the ADORE row (52.65/52.22/54.37/51.11/52.18). **The paper never names its judge/evaluator model** — confirmed; it cites Du et al. 2025 and describes only the RACE weight-vector mechanism.

**Judge-provenance argument:** Confirmed against `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv`. Byte-for-byte matches on all five columns for tavily-research (52.44/52.84/53.59/51.92/49.21), thinkdepthai-deepresearch (52.43/52.02/53.88/52.04/50.12), salesforce-air-deep-research (50.65/50.00/51.09/50.77/50.32), gensee-search-gpt-5 (50.60/50.06/50.76/51.31/49.72), gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch (49.71/…/50.00), langchain-open-deep-research-gpt-5 (49.33/49.80/47.34/51.05/48.99), openai-deepresearch (46.45), claude-research (45.00). None of these rows appear with those values in `leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv`. The inference holds.

**Evaluator change:** Confirmed from the deep_research_bench repo — GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro as the RACE judge (announced May 11, 2026; dual-acceptance window to May 31; full migration June 1, 2026), driven by Google's June 17, 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro. Legacy Gemini-2.5 eval code lives on a separate branch.

**Two defects in the claim as written:**
1. **"~18 months of leaderboard-churn stale" is false.** The paper is dated Jan 26, 2026; today is July 13, 2026. That is ~5.5 months, not 18.
2. **"Every one of those baseline rows is a byte-for-byte match" overreaches.** The paper's row-4 CellCog (51.94/52.17/51.90/51.37/51.94) does *not* match any CellCog row in the Gemini-judged CSV (cellcog-max 56.67, drb_cellcog 55.31). Eight of nine baselines match; CellCog does not. Also note the paper's row *labels* are shuffled relative to the CSV (its "LangChain (GPT-5)" carries gensee-search-gpt-5's numbers; its "LangChain (Tavily)" carries langchain-open-deep-research-gpt-5's numbers).

**Operational conclusion is sound and if anything understated:** 52.65 is not SOTA even on the legacy judge — 22 systems in the Gemini-2.5-Pro CSV score above it (top: qianfan 58.03). On the current GPT-5.5 judge, the leader is cellcog-max at 55.78 and ADORE does not appear at all.

input.reasoning:
Core claim verified against primary sources; two secondary details in the claim are wrong and must be fixed before reuse.

VERIFIED (primary source, arXiv 2601.18267 abs + HTML):
- Paper exists: "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG" (You, Sun, Bora, Li, Goel, Li, Culatana), submitted 26 Jan 2026. Abstract states "ADORE ranks first on DeepResearch Bench (52.65)" and 77.2% on DeepConsult.
- Table 2 reproduces exactly as the claim describes, including ADORE 52.65/52.22/54.37/51.11/52.18 and the full baseline list.
- The paper NEVER names its judge/evaluator model. It cites Du et al. 2025 and describes only the RACE weight-vector mechanism. Confirmed.

VERIFIED (judge-provenance inference), against local /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv:
- Byte-for-byte, all-five-columns matches to the Gemini-2.5-Pro-judged CSV for: tavily-research 52.44/52.84/53.59/51.92/49.21; thinkdepthai-deepresearch 52.43/52.02/53.88/52.04/50.12; salesforce-air-deep-research 50.65/50.00/51.09/50.77/50.32; gensee-search-gpt-5 50.60/50.06/50.76/51.31/49.72; gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.71/49.51/49.45/50.12/50.00; langchain-open-deep-research-gpt-5 49.33/49.80/47.34/51.05/48.99; openai-deepresearch 46.45/46.46/43.73/49.39/47.22; claude-research 45.00/45.34/42.79/47.58/44.66.
- None of those rows carry those values in leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv. The inference that ADORE scored against the Gemini-2.5-Pro judge is sound.

VERIFIED (evaluator change), deep_research_bench repo: GPT-5.5 replaced Gemini-2.5-Pro as the RACE judge (announced 11 May 2026; dual-acceptance window through 31 May; full migration 1 June 2026), prompted by Google's 17 June 2026 deprecation of Gemini-2.5-Pro. Legacy Gemini-2.5 eval code preserved on a separate branch.

OPERATIONAL CONCLUSION HOLDS (and is understated): 52.65 is not SOTA even on the legacy judge — 22 systems in the Gemini-2.5-Pro CSV beat it (top: qianfan_deepresearch_0430 at 58.03). On the current GPT-5.5 judge the leader is cellcog-max at 55.78, and ADORE does not appear. "Do NOT treat 52.65 as SOTA-on-our-evaluator" is correct.

DEFECTS IN THE CLAIM (see `corrected`): the "~18 months" figure is false, and "every one of those baseline rows is a byte-for-byte match" overreaches (CellCog does not match).
input.corrected:
Two corrections to the claim as written:

(1) "~18 months of leaderboard-churn stale" is FALSE. The paper is dated 26 Jan 2026; the current date is 13 July 2026 — that is ~5.5 months, not 18. The staleness that matters is not elapsed time but two discrete events: (a) the RACE judge migrated from Gemini-2.5-Pro to GPT-5.5 (announced 11 May 2026, full migration 1 June 2026, per the Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench repo); and (b) ~22 systems now outscore 52.65 even on the same Gemini-2.5-Pro-judged leaderboard ADORE measured against. Use "the judge changed and the leaderboard moved," not a month count.

(2) "Every one of those baseline rows is a byte-for-byte match" is an overreach. Eight of nine match exactly (tavily-research, thinkdepthai, salesforce-air, gensee-search-gpt-5, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch, langchain-open-deep-research-gpt-5, openai-deepresearch, claude-research). The paper's row-4 "CellCog" (51.94/52.17/51.90/51.37/51.94) matches NO CellCog row in the Gemini-judged CSV (cellcog-max = 56.67, drb_cellcog = 55.31). Also, the paper's row LABELS are shuffled vs. the CSV: its "LangChain (GPT-5)" row carries gensee-search-gpt-5's numbers, and its "LangChain (Tavily)" row carries langchain-open-deep-research-gpt-5's numbers.

Sources: https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18267 (Table 2, abstract); https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench (judge migration); /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/leaderboard_gemini25pro_judge.csv and leaderboard_gpt55_judge.csv (local leaderboard snapshots).
